words.exampleNL: Ik ben blij dat je er bent.
words.exampleEN: I am happy that you are here.
words.audioHint: The 'ij' sounds like 'eye'. Short and crisp.

words.exampleNL: Ze is verdrietig omdat haar kat ziek is.
words.exampleEN: She is sad because her cat is sick.
words.audioHint: Stress on the second syllable: ver-DRIE-tig. The 'ie' sounds like 'ee'.

words.exampleNL: Mijn vader is boos op mij.
words.exampleEN: My father is angry with me.
words.audioHint: Long 'oo' sound, like 'oh'. Not like English 'boos'.

words.exampleNL: Ik ben heel moe na het werk.
words.exampleEN: I am very tired after work.
words.audioHint: The 'oe' sounds like 'oo' in 'moon'. Very short word.

words.exampleNL: Ben je bang voor spinnen?
words.exampleEN: Are you scared of spiders?
words.audioHint: Short 'a'. The 'ng' is like in English 'bang' but the 'g' is softer.

words.exampleNL: Ik ben zenuwachtig voor het examen.
words.exampleEN: I am nervous about the exam.
words.audioHint: From 'zenuw' (nerve) + '-achtig'. Multiple syllables with guttural sounds.

words.exampleNL: Ik ben tevreden met mijn werk.
words.exampleEN: I am satisfied with my work.
words.audioHint: Stress on the second syllable: te-VRE-den.

words.exampleNL: We zijn heel gelukkig samen.
words.exampleEN: We are very happy together.
words.audioHint: The 'g' sounds are guttural. Stress on the second syllable: ge-LUK-kig.

words.exampleNL: Ik ben trots op mijn kinderen.
words.exampleEN: I am proud of my children.
words.audioHint: Short 'o'. Clear 'ts' at the end.

words.exampleNL: Ze is verliefd op haar collega.
words.exampleEN: She is in love with her colleague.
words.audioHint: Stress on the second syllable: ver-LIEFD. Final 'd' sounds like 't'.

words.exampleNL: Hij voelt zich eenzaam in de grote stad.
words.exampleEN: He feels lonely in the big city.
words.audioHint: Long 'ee' like 'ay', then long 'aa' like 'ah'.

words.exampleNL: Ik ben benieuwd naar je verhaal.
words.exampleEN: I am curious about your story.
words.audioHint: Stress on the second syllable: be-NIEUWD. The 'ieuw' is like 'eew'.

words.exampleNL: Ben je jaloers op je zus?
words.exampleEN: Are you jealous of your sister?
words.audioHint: The 'j' sounds like English 'y'. Stress on the second syllable.

words.exampleNL: Ik voel me gestrest door mijn werk.
words.exampleEN: I feel stressed because of my work.
words.audioHint: The 'g' is guttural. Similar to English 'stressed' with prefix.

words.exampleNL: In het weekend voel ik me ontspannen.
words.exampleEN: On the weekend I feel relaxed.
words.audioHint: Stress on the second syllable: ont-SPAN-nen.

words.exampleNL: Ik was verrast door het cadeau.
words.exampleEN: I was surprised by the gift.
words.audioHint: Stress on the second syllable: ver-RAST. Rolled 'r'.

words.exampleNL: Ik ben teleurgesteld in het resultaat.
words.exampleEN: I am disappointed in the result.
words.audioHint: Long compound word. Stress on 'leur': te-LEUR-ge-steld.

words.exampleNL: De kinderen zijn opgewonden over het feest.
words.exampleEN: The children are excited about the party.
words.audioHint: Stress on the third syllable: op-ge-WON-den. The 'g' is guttural.

words.exampleNL: Ik ben verward door de instructies.
words.exampleEN: I am confused by the instructions.
words.audioHint: Stress on the second syllable: ver-WARD. Final 'd' sounds like 't'.

words.exampleNL: Ik weet het zeker.
words.exampleEN: I am sure about it.
words.audioHint: Long 'e' like 'ay'. The 'z' is soft.

words.exampleNL: Ik voel me onzeker over mijn keuze.
words.exampleEN: I feel unsure about my choice.
words.audioHint: Prefix 'on-' (un-) added to 'zeker'.

words.exampleNL: Hoe voel je je vandaag?
words.exampleEN: How do you feel today?
words.audioHint: The 'oe' sounds like 'oo'. The 'v' is soft.

words.exampleNL: Het kind begint te huilen.
words.exampleEN: The child starts to cry.
words.audioHint: The 'ui' diphthong: say 'ow' with rounded lips.

words.exampleNL: We lachen veel samen.
words.exampleEN: We laugh a lot together.
words.audioHint: Short 'a'. The 'ch' is guttural.

words.exampleNL: Ze glimlacht altijd als ze mij ziet.
words.exampleEN: She always smiles when she sees me.
words.audioHint: Compound: 'glim' + 'lachen'. Both the 'g' and 'ch' are guttural.

words.exampleNL: Ik hoop dat het morgen mooi weer wordt.
words.exampleEN: I hope the weather will be nice tomorrow.
words.audioHint: Long 'oo' sound. Note: 'hopen' is infinitive, 'hoop' is first person.

words.exampleNL: Maak je geen zorgen.
words.exampleEN: Don't worry.
words.audioHint: Short 'o'. The 'g' is guttural.

words.exampleNL: Hij schaamt zich voor zijn fout.
words.exampleEN: He is ashamed of his mistake.
words.audioHint: The 'sch' is guttural. Always used reflexively: 'zich schamen'.

words.exampleNL: Ik erger me aan het lawaai.
words.exampleEN: I am annoyed by the noise.
words.audioHint: The 'g' is guttural. Used reflexively for 'to be annoyed'.

words.exampleNL: Ik vertrouw je helemaal.
words.exampleEN: I trust you completely.
words.audioHint: The 'ou' sounds like 'ow' in 'cow'.

words.exampleNL: Wees geduldig, het duurt niet lang meer.
words.exampleEN: Be patient, it won't take much longer.
words.audioHint: The 'g' sounds are guttural. Stress on the second syllable.

words.exampleNL: De kinderen worden ongeduldig.
words.exampleEN: The children are becoming impatient.
words.audioHint: Prefix 'on-' (un-/im-) added to 'geduldig'.

words.exampleNL: Ik ben je heel dankbaar voor je hulp.
words.exampleEN: I am very grateful to you for your help.
words.audioHint: Short 'a' in both syllables. Clear 'nk' in the middle.

words.exampleNL: Ze is altijd zo vrolijk.
words.exampleEN: She is always so cheerful.
words.audioHint: Short 'o' sound in the first syllable, then 'luk'. The 'vr' blend is distinctive.

words.exampleNL: Blijf rustig, alles komt goed.
words.exampleEN: Stay calm, everything will be fine.
words.audioHint: Short 'u'. Final 'g' is guttural.

words.exampleNL: Ik word nerveus van het wachten.
words.exampleEN: I get nervous from waiting.
words.audioHint: Similar to English but with stress on the first syllable.

words.exampleNL: Hij is een bange man.
words.exampleEN: He is a fearful man.
words.audioHint: Inflected form of 'bang'. The 'e' is a schwa sound.

words.exampleNL: Ik ben geschrokken van het harde geluid.
words.exampleEN: I was startled by the loud noise.
words.audioHint: Multiple guttural sounds. Stress on the second syllable: ge-SCHROK-ken.

words.exampleNL: Hij is vandaag in een goed humeur.
words.exampleEN: He is in a good mood today.
words.audioHint: Stress on the second syllable: hu-MEUR. The 'eu' is like 'ur' in 'fur'.

words.exampleNL: Ik heb een goed gevoel hierover.
words.exampleEN: I have a good feeling about this.
words.audioHint: The 'g' is guttural. The 'oe' sounds like 'oo'.

words.exampleNL: Liefde is het belangrijkste in het leven.
words.exampleEN: Love is the most important thing in life.
words.audioHint: The 'ie' sounds like 'ee'. The 'e' at the end is a schwa.

words.exampleNL: Haat lost niets op.
words.exampleEN: Hate solves nothing.
words.audioHint: Long 'aa' sound. Final 't' is crisp.

words.exampleNL: Ze heeft angst voor het donker.
words.exampleEN: She has a fear of the dark.
words.audioHint: Short 'a'. The 'ngst' consonant cluster at the end.

words.exampleNL: Het was een moment van grote vreugde.
words.exampleEN: It was a moment of great joy.
words.audioHint: The 'eu' is like 'ur' in 'fur'. The 'g' is guttural.

words.exampleNL: Ze heeft veel verdriet om het verlies.
words.exampleEN: She has much grief about the loss.
words.audioHint: Stress on the second syllable: ver-DRIET. The 'ie' sounds like 'ee'.

words.exampleNL: Hij probeerde zijn woede te beheersen.
words.exampleEN: He tried to control his anger.
words.audioHint: The 'oe' sounds like 'oo'. The 'e' at the end is a schwa.

words.exampleNL: Ze heeft veel zelfvertrouwen.
words.exampleEN: She has a lot of self-confidence.
words.audioHint: Compound: 'zelf' (self) + 'vertrouwen' (trust/confidence).

words.exampleNL: Ik ben opgelucht dat het examen voorbij is.
words.exampleEN: I am relieved that the exam is over.
words.audioHint: The 'g' and 'ch' are guttural. Stress on the last syllable.

words.exampleNL: Ik raak gefrustreerd als dingen niet lukken.
words.exampleEN: I get frustrated when things don't work out.
words.audioHint: The 'g' is guttural. Similar to English 'frustrated' with prefix.

words.exampleNL: Kinderen zijn van nature nieuwsgierig.
words.exampleEN: Children are naturally curious.
words.audioHint: Compound-style word. 'Nieuws' + 'gierig'. Multiple guttural sounds.

words.exampleNL: Ik verveel me. Laten we iets doen.
words.exampleEN: I am bored. Let's do something.
words.audioHint: Stress on the second syllable: ver-VE-len. Used reflexively for 'to be bored'.

words.exampleNL: Ik geniet van het mooie weer.
words.exampleEN: I enjoy the nice weather.
words.audioHint: The 'g' is guttural. Stress on the second syllable: ge-NIE-ten.

words.exampleNL: Ik mis mijn familie heel erg.
words.exampleEN: I miss my family very much.
words.audioHint: Short 'i'. Double 's' is stressed.

words.exampleNL: Ik hou van je.
words.exampleEN: I love you.
words.audioHint: The 'ou' sounds like 'ow'. In casual speech, 'houden' becomes 'hou'.
